nativeRange.habitat: Reef edges with sand and rubble zones, typically at 15-60 m depth
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-Pacific reef-edge sand and rubble flats

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate 5-8 cm deep for burrowing

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of frozen mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, and quality marine flakes/pellets. Small, frequent feedings throughout the day tend to suit this active mid-water swimmer.

summary: A hardy, brilliantly yellow reef-edge wrasse from the Indo-Pacific. Generally peaceful and reef-safe with corals, but has been known to pick at ornamental shrimp and fan worms.
careNotes: The Yellow Coris Wrasse (Halichoeres chrysus) is generally considered a beginner-friendly marine wrasse, though a few traits push it toward intermediate care. A tight-fitting lid is essential — like most wrasses, it has been known to jump from open-top aquariums. A fine sand bed of at least 5-8 cm is required, as the fish burrows into the substrate to sleep at night and when stressed; without it, the fish may bash itself against the glass or refuse to settle. A minimum tank of around 50-60 US gallons (roughly 190-225 L) is generally advised, and a 200 L or larger aquarium with open swimming space along the front is a sensible target given the species' active mid-water foraging. The Yellow Coris Wrasse is commonly considered reef-safe with corals and is welcomed by many reef keepers as a natural predator of pyramidellid snails, flatworms, bristleworms, and other reef pests; however, it cannot distinguish desirable from undesirable invertebrates, so ornamental shrimp, fan worms, and small snails are at risk. Conspecific aggression tends to be moderate — keeping the fish singly or as an established male/female pair is advisable, and adding a second adult to an existing setup later carries real risk. Halichoeres wrasses are protogynous hermaphrodites (female-first sex changers), and large dominant individuals can become bullish toward similar-shaped tankmates.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form pairs and spawn pelagically in the wild, with planktonic larvae. Captive breeding of Halichoeres chrysus is not commercially established; the planktonic larval stage is the main hurdle.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Yellow Wrasse care profile page used as primary husbandry source (5 in adult, 50 gal min, peaceful, reef safe, carnivore, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae), max length (12 cm TL), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean to Western Pacific: Australia, Indonesia, Japan, Solomon Islands, Tonga), depth 2-70 m typically 15-60 m, and reef-edge sand/rubble habitat. Temperature 24-27°C preferred 25.2-28.9°C — averaged with LiveAquaria's 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C) to a centered 23-27°C range.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Third source for tank-size cross-check: confirms 50-60 gal minimum, jumping risk, sand-burrowing behavior, and conspecific aggression. Tank size averaged at 55 gal then biased to the larger nice value of 200 L per playbook tank-size rule.

additional.name: Reef Chasers Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-check for reef-safe status with caveats on ornamental invertebrates and conspecific aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: The Yellow Coris Wrasse spawns pelagically (releasing eggs and sperm directly into the water column) in the wild, producing planktonic larvae that drift with the currents. Captive breeding has not been established in home aquariums.
